title: Module A: Security foundations
summary: A from-zero tour of cybersecurity fundamentals: why security became a systemic necessity, the three scopes of the definition (user -> system -> nation), the core vocabulary (danger, threat, asset, vulnerability, risk), safety vs security vs dependability and resilience, the CIA triad and related security properties, cryptographic countermeasures, privacy vs data protection vs secrecy and the GDPR, anonymization vs pseudonymization, access control models, and how attacks are described with the threat chain, kill chain, CWE/CVE, real vulnerability and ransomware case studies, the national cyber architecture, threat-intelligence sharing, and MITRE ATT&CK.
objectives: Explain why cybersecurity is a systemic problem and describe cyberspace (and national cyberspace) as a socio-technical system shaped by complexity, software scale and pervasiveness
State the three scopes of cybersecurity definitions (user -> system -> nation) and the common core (CIA, resilience, risk reduction)
Define and correctly distinguish danger, threat, asset, vulnerability and risk (R = S x P), and run the asset-inventory -> threats -> controls -> residual-risk loop

Distinguish safety, security and dependability; compare safety and security on accidentality vs intentionality, cardinality, failure prevention (fault-tolerant vs vulnerability-tolerant) and standards; explain the three threat chains and (Safety & Security)-by-Design

State the CIA triad and the additional properties authenticity, accountability and non-repudiation, match attacker capabilities (read/modify/block/inject) to violated properties, and explain availability metrics (uptime, MTBF, MTTR, five nines)
Explain symmetric and asymmetric cryptography, hashes, MACs and digital signatures, and Kerckhoffs's principle

Distinguish privacy, data protection and secrecy; interpret GDPR principles and organizational duties; distinguish anonymization vs pseudonymization; and read the Italian and international classification levels
Distinguish authentication from authorization and compare DAC, MAC, RBAC and ABAC access control models

Describe attacks using the threat chain, the CWE/CVE distinction, real vulnerabilities (Heartbleed, Log4Shell, EternalBlue), the cyber kill chain, threat-actor classes (state/cybercrime/hacktivism) and major ransomware incidents
Explain attacker-defender asymmetry, the national cyber architecture and structured threat-intelligence sharing (CSIRT/ISAC, STIX/MISP, IOC/TTP)
Use MITRE ATT&CK tactics/techniques/procedures for defensive mapping and analyze Stuxnet as an IT+OT+physical campaign

blocks.text: 1. Why cybersecurity exists

Digital services give us **convenience, speed and reach**: online banking, shopping, healthcare portals, smart devices. But the same infrastructure that scales convenience also scales **failures, attacks and disruption**: fraud, ransomware, outages, leaked data, deep fakes.

The lesson of the course: *one click can unlock convenience... or unleash chaos.* Security is the discipline that tries to keep the first without the second.

Computing did not always look like this. A quick timeline shows how risk grew with connectivity:

- **1940s-60s - Isolated computing.** Machines like ENIAC were centralized and required physical access. Users were few, trusted and co-located; failures were local and contained. Security assumptions were *physical, not digital*.
- **1948 - Stored-program concept (Manchester Baby).** Programs and data share the same memory, so instructions become modifiable digital objects. This enables software flexibility, reusable logic and reprogrammable machines - but also software exploitation. *Flexibility is the root of both innovation and vulnerability.*
- **1969 - Networking (ARPANET).** ARPANET introduced packet switching and remote collaboration; trust assumptions expand beyond a single room. *Distance stops being protection.*
- **1971 - Creeper.** The first self-propagating program (Bob Thomas, BBN) ran on ARPANET. Not malicious, but it showed code can move autonomously across a network - *code mobility is a security problem.*
- **1972 - Reaper.** Ray Tomlinson (inventor of email) wrote it to locate and remove Creeper: the first example of **active defense**. Attack and defense start to **co-evolve** - *defense exists because threats scale.*
- **1980s - Commercial defense.** PCs spread, viruses spread via floppy disks, the first commercial antivirus products appear (e.g. McAfee). *Security follows adoption* and becomes a market.
- **1991 - World Wide Web (Tim Berners-Lee: HTML, URL, HTTP).** Browsers lower the entry barrier; mass adoption begins and digital identity starts to matter. *Usability enables adoption... and mass exposure.*
- **1990s - The world goes online.** The Internet becomes public and commercial; personal data moves online; identity theft and cybercrime emerge; attacks become **economically motivated**; firewalls and antivirus become standard. *When value moves online, crime follows.*
- **2000s - Threats professionalize.** Organized cybercrime and **Crime-as-a-Service** appear; governments react; security becomes strategic - a national concern.
- **2007 - today - Pervasive connectivity.** With the iPhone and cloud computing, the Internet becomes mobile, always-on and embedded in physical systems. The attack surface explodes and cybersecurity becomes a **systemic problem**.

The complex, dynamic **socio-technical** environment resulting from the interaction of people, software, services and networks connected to the Internet. It is shaped by human behavior and automation and governed by technical, legal and social rules (ISO/IEC 27000:2014 & 27032:2012). In cyberspace **interdependencies dominate** and **failures propagate** - cybersecurity exists because cyberspace exists.

The segment of the global network that falls within a nation's legal and administrative purview. It is interlinked with global cyberspace but distinct in its **governance**, under national laws and regulations, and shaped by the country's economic, cultural and political context. It includes all devices, software and data within the nation's boundaries and its interconnected citizens, businesses and government. National cyberspace is where security becomes a matter of **sovereignty, law and coordinated response** (R. Baldoni, *Charting Digital Sovereignty*).

blocks.title: The core idea of Module A
blocks.text: Connectivity turned isolated systems into **interdependent ecosystems**, so a single vulnerability can affect thousands or millions of systems at once. Security is therefore no longer *local* - it is **systemic**. Cybersecurity is a response to systemic digital risk: digitalization made connectivity the default, connectivity created interdependence, interdependence amplified both failures and attacks, and security moved from a technical issue to a systemic necessity.

A **system** here is not one device. It is a set of interacting components that perform a function, exchange information and depend on each other: hardware, software, networks, data, **users/operators, and procedures**.

Two families matter:
- **IT (Information Technology):** manages data, computation, communication (servers, databases, cloud).
- **OT (Operational Technology):** controls physical processes (industrial controllers, sensors, actuators).

Modern infrastructure mixes IT and OT, which creates new dependencies, new attack paths and **real-world physical consequences**.

blocks.term: Three definitions of cybersecurity (user -> system -> nation)
blocks.text:
Many definitions exist; the course highlights three, at increasing scope:
- **Operational / user-centric (UK NCSC):** cybersecurity is how individuals and organisations *reduce the risk of cyber attack* - protecting the devices we use and the services we access from theft or damage, and preventing unauthorised access to personal information.
- **System-level:** the set of activities needed to protect networks, information systems, services and electronic communications from cyber threats, ensuring their **confidentiality, integrity, availability (CIA)** and **resilience**.
- **National-scale (ISO/IEC 27000/27032):** the practice that allows an entity (organization, citizen, nation) to protect its **physical assets** and the **CIA of its information** from threats coming from cyberspace - connecting cybersecurity to national assets and cyber-physical impact.

Despite different scopes, all converge on a shared **common core: CIA, resilience, and risk reduction.**

Cyberspace is often called *the most complex thing man has ever built*: networks of networks, software/protocol stratification (hardware, firmware, OS, middleware, applications, cloud/services, human interaction) and heterogeneous devices. **Complexity creates hidden dependencies, small changes can have systemic effects, and security failures often emerge gradually until they become visible.**

Software scale makes formal verification infeasible: the **Linux kernel exceeds 30 million lines of code (LoC)**, full Linux distributions exceed **100 million LoC**, Windows is roughly **30-45 million LoC**, large enterprise platforms reach hundreds of millions, and entire ecosystems reach billions of LoC. The result: *systems are vulnerable* - more than **2,800 security vulnerabilities** have been found in the Linux kernel alone, about **900** of them rated high or critical. Cyberspace is also **pervasive**: it now extends into robotics, 5G, always-on devices and daily life, and pervasiveness is a key driver of attack-surface explosion.

Cybersecurity problems scale with interdependence and must be addressed at **all levels** - **individual, organization, national, international** - because the higher the interdependence, the higher the systemic impact. It is therefore a **coordination problem across layers**: the *human level* (users, workers, admins), the *organizational level* (businesses, operators), the *national level* (defense, governance, law) and the *global ecosystem* (international supply chains, Internet routing).

Scale of the field: recent trends report **+27% severe incidents globally in 2024 vs 2023**, with **Italy accounting for about 10% of global attacks** (Rapporto CLUSIT 2025). The global cybersecurity **market** is worth on the order of **$227-248 billion (2025-2026)** and is forecast to roughly double within the decade. Europe, however, is fragmented and underinvested: in 2021 EU cybersecurity investment was about **814 million euro versus about 15 billion euro in the US** - fragmentation that translates into strategic dependency and reduced technological sovereignty. (Attack maps such as Check Point or Kaspersky are *visualization tools, not measurements of risk or impact.*)
2. The vocabulary of risk

To talk precisely about security we need a small set of terms the professor defines carefully. A note for Italian speakers: the word **Sicurezza** is broad and covers several distinct English terms (safety, security, dependability). The trickiest distinction is **danger vs threat**.

Two complementary definitions:
- The **state of being free from dangers or threats.**
- (NIST SP 800-160) The **freedom from those conditions that can cause loss of assets with unacceptable consequences.** This implies an **asset view**: *no assets -> no meaningful security discussion.* Stakeholders must clearly define the assets to which security applies and the consequences against which it is assessed.

**Danger** is an intrinsic property of a factor (environment, material, equipment, method, process) with the potential to generate a harmful event - e.g. a high-voltage cabinet is dangerous *regardless of who interacts with it*. Danger exists even with **no malicious actor**, and equals the *possibility of suffering harm or injury*.

**Threat** introduces **intention** (a hostile agent): a statement of intent to inflict harm. In cyber terms (NIST), a threat is any circumstance or event that could adversely impact operations, assets, individuals, other organizations or the Nation through an information system via unauthorized access, destruction, disclosure, modification, or denial of service.

Both depend on context: for a GPS receiver, civilian threats include location-privacy violations and navigation-data manipulation, while military threats include adversary signal **spoofing** and strategic **denial** of navigation signals.

Any item of value owned by an organization that can be monetized. Assets are **tangible** (hardware, computing platforms, network devices) or **intangible** (data, information, software, trademarks, copyright, intellectual property, image, reputation). Intangible assets are often the primary target of cyberattacks. The loss of an asset always has an **impact**, evaluated for that specific asset along four dimensions: **value, criticality, irreplaceability and mission role**. Impact analysis turns an 'asset' into a 'decision priority'.
Risk combines how bad an outcome is with how likely it is:

$R = S \times P$, where $S$ is the **severity (impact)** of the consequence and $P$ is the **probability** of occurrence. Many standards say the same thing (UNI EN ISO 12100, OHSAS 18001, the Italian *Testo Unico*), and NIST SP 800-30 writes it as **Risk = Likelihood x Impact**. More generally (Renn): risk is *the possibility that human actions or events lead to consequences that impact what humans value.* **Cyber risk** is the possibility of damage (financial loss, service interruption or reputational damage) suffered by an organization as a result of a malfunction in its information systems.

blocks.title: Two cyber-risk examples
blocks.text:
- **Healthcare service.** Asset: clinical data and service availability. Threat: ransomware. Impact: service disruption and patient-safety risk. Cyber risk: unacceptable disruption to essential healthcare.
- **University information system.** Asset: personal data and academic records. Threat: unauthorized access / data breach. Impact: privacy violations and reputational damage. Cyber risk: loss of trust and regulatory consequences.
The operational loop of risk-based security management:

steps.label: Inventory
steps.text: Prepare a detailed **asset inventory** - you cannot protect what you have not listed.

steps.label: Analyze threats
steps.text: For each asset, analyze the possible threats - these are the **risks** to consider.

steps.label: Protect
steps.text: Prepare appropriate protections and countermeasures (controls).

steps.label: Residual risk
steps.text: Evaluate the **residual risk** that remains, and possibly **insure** it (cyber-insurance policies now cover residual cyber risk).

blocks.text: National agencies provide reference methods to standardize risk treatment. In Italy, **AgID** and the National Cybersecurity Authority (**ACN**) provide an online cyber-risk management tool that lets organizations assess risk, document treatment plans and monitor risk-reduction over time. It is based on **ISO 31000** (*Risk management - Principles and guidelines*; Italian UNI ISO 31000) and on **IRAM2** (Information Risk Assessment Methodology 2), developed by the Information Security Forum (ISF).

blocks.title: Exam trap: danger is not threat, and a CVE is not a risk
blocks.text:
Two recurring mistakes:
- **Danger** is a passive property; a **threat** requires a hostile, intentional agent. Saying 'a malicious hacker is a danger' is imprecise - they are a threat.
- A high risk requires **both** high severity *and* non-negligible probability. A catastrophic-but-impossible event is not a high risk; neither is a likely-but-trivial one.

question.prompt: A lithium battery can catch fire. No attacker is involved. In the course's vocabulary, the battery's fire potential is best described as a:

options.content: Threat, because it can cause harm
Danger, because it is an intrinsic harmful property independent of any malicious actor
Vulnerability, because it is a flaw

question.explanation: The fire potential exists regardless of any actor, which is exactly the definition of **danger**. It is not a **threat** (no hostile intention/agent), not a **vulnerability** (which is an exploitable flaw in a system), and not a **risk** (risk is severity x probability, a quantified combination, not the property itself).
question.theory: Danger is an intrinsic harmful property; a threat needs a hostile agent.

blocks.text: 2b. Safety, security and dependability

*Sicurezza* spans several distinct concerns. The course separates **what we protect** into safety (people, environment, objects) and security (computers, information, cyberspace), and unifies both under **dependability**. It also defines **computer security** precisely before moving to cyber-specific threats.

The property of a system that lets it operate, normally or abnormally, **without danger of causing human injury or death and without damaging its environment.** Probabilistically: the probability that a system will either perform its function correctly **or discontinue its function in a way that does not disrupt other systems or endanger people**. The slogan: *safe systems may fail, but they must fail safely.* Safety-critical systems are governed by strict international standards (IEC, ISO, aerospace) that encode best practice and the state of the art; **compliance** provides technical and legal protection, and lack of compliance in safety-critical domains is a serious engineering fault.

**Computer security** deals with the *prevention and detection of unauthorized actions* by users of a computer system; **authorization** is its cornerstone. (NIST NISTIR 7298) It is the set of measures and controls that ensure the **CIA** of information-system assets - hardware, software, firmware and information being processed, stored and communicated. It requires a **security policy**: the rule set stating who (or what) is authorized to perform which actions and when. Example: in a university system, professors may modify the grades of students in their own courses, while students may only *view* their own grades, not modify them.

A system can be compromised **physically or logically**, so protection needs both:
- **Physical security** prevents unauthorized *physical* interaction - access control to buildings/offices/desks, environmental protection (fire, natural events) and infrastructure continuity (electrical continuity).
- **Logical security** prevents unauthorized *logical* actions - perimeter and system protection (endpoint/server), operational continuity via a **Business Continuity Plan (BCP)**, and **backup & disaster recovery** with primary/backup links.

The Italian AgID maps the CIA triad to **RID**: Riservatezza, Integrita, Disponibilita. **Cybersecurity** is the part dealing with **intentional, intelligent, adaptive threats acting through cyberspace** - what makes it different is the environment: attacks can be remote, spread very quickly across interconnected systems, and a single vulnerability may hit millions of systems simultaneously.

blocks.term: Failure and the three threat chains
blocks.text:
A **failure** is the impossibility of meeting a requirement, objective or performance measure (e.g. an infusion pump delivering the wrong dosage due to a sensor fault). Different communities describe how failures emerge with different **threat chains**:
- **Safety community - 3-universe model (accidental causality):** **Fault** (physical domain) -> **Error** (information domain) -> **Failure** (user domain). Example (ABS brakes): a damaged wheel-speed sensor (fault) -> wrong speed measurement (error) -> wrong braking logic, unsafe braking (failure). Without tolerance mechanisms, a fault may lead to an error and then a failure.
- **Software community (human error):** **Error** (a human action producing an incorrect result) -> **Bug/Fault** (an incorrect step/process/data in a program) -> **Failure** (inability to perform required functions at runtime, when the faulty code executes).
- **Security community (adversarial causality):** **Weakness** (a flaw/limitation in design or implementation) -> **Vulnerability** (a weakness an attacker realizes can be exploited) -> **Attack** (a concrete exploitation). Example: a login form that does not validate input (weakness) allows SQL injection (vulnerability), which the attacker exploits to bypass authentication (attack).
(Reference: Avizienis, Laprie, Randell, Landwehr, *Basic Concepts and Taxonomy of Dependable and Secure Computing*, IEEE TDSC 2004.)

The course compares **safety vs security** along five axes:

- **Conflicting requirements.** Example: a safety rule says 'doors shall open when the car is flipped', solved with a roof pressure sensor - but that creates a security hole (unauthorized entry by jumping on the car). *A system can be safe and insecure at the same time.* The only sound answer is **(Safety & Security)-by-Design**: security cannot be retrofitted once systems are deployed.
- **Accidentality vs intentionality.** In safety the threat is always **accidental** - endogenous (component failure), exogenous (atmospheric events, electromagnetic noise) or human-but-unintentional (design/manufacturing/testing error). In security the threat is **intentional**, carried by a malicious agent.
- **Cardinality.** Safety failures arise from limited combinations of accidental events and manifest sporadically in only a few systems at a time. In security, modern systems share common software components, so a **single vulnerability can compromise thousands or millions of systems simultaneously** at large scale.
- **Failure prevention.** Safety engineering prevents failures from **accidental faults**; security engineering prevents failures from **malicious exploitation of vulnerabilities**.
- **The role of standards.** A significant **convergence** of safety and security standards has been achieved (illustrated by projects such as AMASS across IEC/ISO/aerospace).

Both fields design systems that keep working despite problems, but the threat differs:
- **Safety - Fault-Tolerant Architectures** exploit **redundancy**: **time redundancy** (repeat an operation and compare results), **information redundancy** (extra bits via Error Detection And Correction - EDAC - codes, used in comms, storage and spacecraft), **functional redundancy** (different algorithms/implementations so the same fault is unlikely to hit all), and **structural redundancy** (identical modules in parallel with a **voting** mechanism, e.g. **N-Modular Redundancy (NMR)** such as **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)**, used in spacecraft to tolerate radiation-induced transient faults / single-event upsets).
- **Security - Vulnerability-Tolerant Architectures** stay secure even when vulnerabilities exist, via **defense in depth, isolation/sandboxing, least privilege, monitoring and intrusion detection**, plus CIA-specific controls: confidentiality (encryption, access control), integrity (hashes, digital signatures, message authentication), availability (redundancy, replication, resilient architectures).

The property of a system that allows reliance to be **justifiably** placed on the service it delivers. It is broken down into three elements: **Attributes** (the properties of a dependable system), **Threats** (events/conditions that can affect dependability) and **Means** (mechanisms to increase dependability). Its **attributes** are: **Reliability** (performs its function consistently over time), **Safety** (causes no harm), **Security** (protection against malicious actions, i.e. confidentiality, integrity, availability) and **Maintainability** (ability to repair and restore the system efficiently).

The ability of a system to **prepare** for adverse events, **absorb** disruption while maintaining essential functions, **recover** within a mission-acceptable time, and **adapt** to evolving threats and conditions (adapted from NIST SP 800-39 and SP 800-160). Resilience helps an organisation protect against cyber risks, limit the severity of attacks and ensure its continued **survival despite an attack** - which is why it is part of the common core of cybersecurity alongside CIA and risk reduction.
3. Security properties: the CIA triad and friends

Pervasive cyberspace produces consequences worth naming: **Big Data** (massive heterogeneous streams collected continuously), **Datacracy** (decisions and power increasingly depend on data access and analytics) and a **data-driven society** (services and institutions optimize on data and models). The amount of data we produce keeps doubling, and with the Internet of Things tens of billions of devices come online. Security implication: *more data + more connectivity => more exposure, more inference, more impact.* At scale, small leaks become **mass surveillance and mass exploitation**.

Before properties, one subtle distinction: **data vs information**.

- **Data** are raw, unprocessed items: 'ZIP = 13053', 'Age = 31', a GPS coordinate, a timestamp.
- **Information** is the meaning inferred from data + context: 'Bob has cancer', 'Bob's home address'. Information is **more general than data**: data convey information, but information can be revealed *without* revealing data - e.g. a statistical summary like 'average age in Ward X is 31' can identify a specific person if the ward is small or auxiliary knowledge exists. This is an **inference attack** (e.g. **linkage**: combining quasi-identifiers like age + ZIP with an external dataset to learn a sensitive attribute), and it is why scale makes privacy a systemic problem.

blocks.title: Machine learning makes inference worse
blocks.text: ML models can infer **sensitive attributes from seemingly harmless data** by exploiting statistical patterns. Named attack types: **membership inference**, **attribute inference** and **model inversion**. Real research examples (Conti et al.): inferring user actions from encrypted Android traffic, inferring what you type from keyboard sound over VoIP ('Don't Skype & Type!'), and inferring PIN codes from the sound/timing of key presses.

The three security pillars (Italian: **RID**), which apply to both **data and services**:
- **Confidentiality** - prevent unauthorized disclosure. Failure = a *data breach*. Enforced with encryption, access control, authentication.
- **Integrity** - prevent (or detect) unauthorized modification or destruction. Two scopes: **data integrity** (data/programs changed only in an authorized way, e.g. a transaction amount or medical dosage) and **systems integrity** (the system operates unimpaired and free from unauthorized manipulation, e.g. OS kernel, firmware; **Trusted Computing** studies how to detect if a system has been compromised).
- **Availability** - ensure timely, reliable access to data and services. Threatened by DoS/DDoS, ransomware outages, and physical disruption such as power failures.

Formally, availability is the probability **A(t)** that the system functions correctly at instant t. Operational metrics:
- **Uptime** - percentage of time the service is operational and accessible.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** - average time a system operates correctly between consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** - average time to detect, fix and restore after a failure.

Services publish availability guarantees in **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**. Cloud providers often advertise **'five nines' = 99.999% uptime**, which is roughly **five minutes of downtime per year.**

Three properties beyond CIA:
- **Authenticity** - the message/source is genuine and verifiable. Answers: *'Did this really come from who it claims?'* Mechanisms: digital signatures, MACs, authenticated protocols.
- **Accountability** - actions of an entity (user, process, device) can be **uniquely traced** to that entity. Supports incident response/forensics, auditing/compliance and deterrence. Mechanisms: secure logging, tamper-evident audit trails, identity binding (authentication + access control).
- **Non-repudiation** - a party cannot **falsely deny** an action, backed by **cryptographic evidence** that the action occurred (e.g. a sender cannot deny sending a message; a signer cannot deny signing). Mechanisms: digital signatures, trusted timestamping, secure key management, strong identity binding.

blocks.title: Accountability vs non-repudiation
blocks.text: They sound similar but differ in strength: **accountability enables tracing** (via secure logging and audit trails), while **non-repudiation provides cryptographic proof** that an action occurred and cannot be denied (via digital signatures and trusted timestamping).

**Authentication (AuthN)** verifies **who** is requesting access (person or system), using one or more factors:
- something you **know** (password, PIN),
- something you **have** (token, smart card),
- something you **are** (biometrics).

Stronger practice combines factors = **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. (Authentication answers *who you are*; **authorization (AuthZ)** decides *what you can do* - see section 5.)

A clean way to see CIA is the **source -> destination -> attacker** communication model. A message or service flows from Source S to Destination D, and an attacker A can perform four actions, each violating a specific property:

- **Read** (eavesdrop / *stealing*) -> breaks **Confidentiality**. Examples: sniffing data sent in clear, accessing a vulnerable database. Controls: encryption (in transit/at rest), access control, network segmentation.
- **Modify** (tamper / *corrupting*) -> breaks **Integrity**. Example: redirecting a bank transfer (the attacker may be in the browser as malware or on the network as a **Man-in-the-Middle**). Controls: MACs, digital signatures, secure protocols such as TLS.
- **Block** (deny / *inhibiting*) -> breaks **Availability**. Examples: DoS/DDoS, disruption of critical infrastructure. Controls: redundancy, rate limiting, distributed infrastructures, incident response.
- **Inject** (forge / *forging*) -> breaks **Authenticity**. Examples: phishing, software supply-chain attacks (weak crypto design, stolen keys, implementation flaws). Controls: digital signatures, secure key management, verification.

Countermeasures are **layered** (defense-in-depth): attacks can target hardware, software, networks and people. The course focuses on three message-protection tools - **Encryption** (confidentiality), **MACs** (integrity/authenticity) and **Digital signatures** (integrity/authenticity/non-repudiation).

**Encryption** transforms a message using an **algorithm** and a **cryptographic key** so it is unintelligible to unauthorized readers; the result is **ciphertext**. It provides **confidentiality** and protects data **in transit** (network) and **at rest** (storage). Crucially, the security of a cryptographic system depends on **key management, not on the secrecy of the algorithm** - this is **Kerckhoffs's principle**, which is what lets cryptographic systems be publicly scrutinized and trusted.

- **Symmetric:** one shared secret key $K$ for both operations - **Encrypt:** $C = E(K, M)$; **Decrypt:** $M = D(K, C)$. Very efficient, so used for **bulk data** (files, disks, network traffic). Main challenge: the **key-exchange problem** - getting $K$ to both parties securely at scale.
- **Asymmetric (public-key):** each entity has a **public key** (disclosed) and a **private key** (kept secret), used in a complementary way. **Public-key encryption** gives confidentiality (encrypt with the recipient's *public* key, decrypt with their *private* key). **Digital signatures** give integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation (sign with the sender's *private* key, verify with the *public* key).
- In real systems, asymmetric crypto securely exchanges a **session key**, which then encrypts the session's data with fast symmetric encryption.

blocks.term: Hash functions, MACs and digital signatures
blocks.text:
- **Cryptographic hash function:** takes a message $M$ of any length and returns a fixed-length **digest** $h = H(M)$. Desired properties: **one-way** (hard to reverse) and **collision-resistant** (hard to find two messages with the same digest). A hash lets a receiver detect **accidental** changes (corruption, transmission/storage errors) when the attacker cannot alter both $M$ and $h$ - but a hash **alone is not enough against an active attacker**.
- **MAC (Message Authentication Code):** the sender computes a tag from the message using a **shared secret key**; the receiver recomputes it and checks for a match. A match means *no one altered the data*; a mismatch means it was altered. Provides **authenticated integrity** (integrity + authenticity).
- **Digital signature:** hash the data, then encrypt the digest with the signer's **private key**; the verifier hashes the received data and compares it with the digest recovered using the signer's **public key**. Provides integrity + authenticity + **non-repudiation** (public verification).

blocks.title: Cryptographic countermeasures in one glance
blocks.text:
The course covers three message-protection tools:
- **Encryption** -> *confidentiality*. Symmetric uses one shared key $K$ ($C=E(K,M)$, $M=D(K,C)$); asymmetric uses a public/private key pair. Security depends on **key management, not secrecy of the algorithm** (Kerckhoffs's principle).
- **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** -> *integrity + authenticity* using a shared secret key. A plain hash alone only catches accidental errors, not an active attacker.
- **Digital signature** -> *integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation*. Sign with the sender's **private** key, verify with the **public** key.

question.prompt: An attacker performs a Man-in-the-Middle attack and silently changes the destination account number of a bank transfer in transit. Which security property is primarily violated, and which control is designed to stop it?

options.content: Integrity - prevented by a MAC or digital signature

question.explanation: Changing data in transit is unauthorized **modification**, which violates **Integrity**; a MAC or digital signature lets the receiver detect the tampering. Encryption protects confidentiality (reading), not modification. Availability concerns blocking the flow, not altering it. Logging supports accountability after the fact but does not prevent the change.
question.theory: Tampering in transit breaks integrity; MACs/signatures detect it.

blocks.text: 4. Privacy, data protection and secrecy

Lecture A3 studied **how** to protect information (CIA and related properties, plus mechanisms like encryption, authentication and access control). Information protection asks **why, for whom and under which constraints** information must be protected, and distinguishes three dimensions: **privacy, data protection and secrecy**.

- **Privacy** - protects the **individual's private sphere**: private life and autonomy, communications, behavioural patterns (habits, profiling) and identity construction (freedom from unjustified surveillance). Defined by *fundamental rights*. *Privacy protects the person, not just the data.* In Italy (GPDP) it covers both the right to keep third parties from accessing what is 'mine' and the right to **personal identity** (integrity and accuracy of one's data when processed by others).
- **Data protection** - governs the **processing of personal data**. Defined by legal/governance frameworks (the EU **GDPR**).
- **Secrecy** - protects **sensitive institutional or state information** to prevent harm from disclosure. The rules are set by *institutional authority*, mission needs and public-interest requirements (national security, intelligence).

*Privacy requires mechanisms, not just intentions.* It typically requires: **data minimization** (collect only what is strictly necessary for a defined purpose), **purpose limitation** (use data only for the originally specified purpose), **informed consent**, **control over access and disclosure**, and **limitations on retention** (keep data only as long as needed).

**The role of technology cuts both ways.** Technology enables large-scale data collection, large-scale **inference** (deducing information that was not explicitly provided), automated **profiling** (predicting behavior, preferences, reliability or risk) and **mass surveillance** (systematic large-scale monitoring of many people at once). But it also enables encryption, access control and privacy-enhancing technologies such as **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)**. *Technology must protect user privacy from attacks brought by technology itself.*

Digital systems generate **explicit data** (what we intentionally share), **implicit data** (metadata, logs, device fingerprints, click patterns, timing, location traces) and **derived data** (inferences/profiling). Risk arises from **data aggregation, cross-dataset correlation and predictive analytics**. **Platform power** is an asymmetry: platforms know us better than we know ourselves, aggregating across services to predict and influence choices via ranking, nudging and targeting.

blocks.title: Accessible != Public != Legitimately usable
blocks.text: That data is technically reachable does not make it lawful to use. **Accessible** = reachable; **public** = intentionally disclosed; **legitimately usable** = allowed by law and purpose. 'Publicly accessible' is *not* the same as 'OK to scrape, merge and profile'. **Availability != authorization** (lawful basis + purpose matter).

blocks.title: The privacy paradox
blocks.text: People *say* they value privacy but accept default settings, click 'Agree' to get access fast, and share more than intended. Why: **information asymmetry** (users don't know what is collected/inferred), **bounded rationality** (costs are immediate, harms are delayed/uncertain) and **dark patterns** (interfaces add friction to refusing and nudge accepting). Consequence: consent becomes a **low-quality, weak signal** of real preferences. *If a service is free, the price is you and your data.*

Since **25 May 2018**, EU Regulation **2016/679 (GDPR)** is directly applicable in all Member States, governing the processing and free movement of personal data. Goals: **harmonization** across the EU and **protection** in the digital economy. Key idea: **accountability** - organizations must *prove* compliance. The grounding is in fundamental rights: the **Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Art. 12)** against arbitrary interference with privacy, and the **EU Charter of Fundamental Rights** (Art. 7 - respect for private life; Art. 8 - protection of personal data, requiring fair processing for specified purposes, a lawful basis/consent, access and rectification rights, and oversight by an independent authority). With GDPR, **security becomes a design constraint, not an optional add-on.**

blocks.title: From GDPR principles to organizational duties
blocks.text: GDPR principles become concrete engineering duties:

steps.label: Lawful basis & purpose
steps.text: Personal data must be collected for a clear, legitimate, specified purpose.

steps.label: Minimization & retention
steps.text: Collect only what is necessary and keep it only as long as needed.

steps.label: Access control & protection
steps.text: Restrict access and secure data in storage and in transit.

steps.label: Transparency & data-subject rights
steps.text: Inform users and let them access, correct or erase their data where applicable.

steps.label: Documentation & incident readiness
steps.text: Compliance must be demonstrable; breaches must be detected, assessed and managed.

blocks.text: (ISO/IEC 27040) A security breach that accidentally or unlawfully results in the **destruction, loss, modification, unauthorized disclosure of, or access to** personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. A breach can hit any CIA property: **confidentiality** (a leaked database), **integrity** (a tampered medical record), **availability** (ransomware encryption). Impact depends on sector, data sensitivity, detection time and response readiness, and includes **direct costs** (incident response, forensics, legal, recovery) and **indirect costs** (downtime, lost trust, lost business). IBM's *Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025* puts the global average **well over \$4 million**.

**Protection mechanisms work together** across the data lifecycle (none alone is enough - privacy and data protection need **layered controls**):
- **Access control** - restricts *who* can access/use data and under what conditions; enforces authentication (who you are), authorization (what you can do) and accountability (what you did).
- **Encryption** - protects the data *representation* (confidentiality) at rest and in transit, but does **not** regulate how data is used once decrypted - so *encryption != privacy*.
- **Logging** - automatically generated event records (log files, audit trails, centralized logging platforms) that enable traceability, anomaly detection and incident investigation. Logging is a **detective control, not a preventive control.**
- **Anonymization** and **pseudonymization** act on identifiers / identifiability (below).

- **Anonymization** - processes personal data so individuals can **no longer be identified in any way**: removes/alters direct identifiers and reduces linkability. Its effectiveness depends on the **surrounding environment** (what other data and auxiliary information exist). If effective it is **irreversible** and the data fall **outside GDPR** (no longer personal data).
- **Pseudonymization** - replaces identifiers with artificial **tokens**, **reversible** only with additional information kept separately under technical/organisational safeguards. Data **remain personal data** and stay under GDPR; it reduces breach impact and supports **privacy-by-design**, but does not remove regulatory obligations.

Comparison: anonymization acts on *identifiability* (no personal data if effective; goal: remove identifiability); pseudonymization acts on *identifiers* (still personal data; goal: reduce exposure).

**Secrecy** is a **governance mechanism for managing high-impact risk.** It protects information whose disclosure would harm collective interests - national security, strategic infrastructure, military operations, intelligence - so it protects **institutions, missions and the State**, not individual rights. It supports national defense by protecting strategic capabilities, denying adversarial intelligence gathering (information *denied* to an adversary is often as valuable as information possessed) and reducing systemic vulnerability - but **excessive secrecy reduces transparency**, so democratic systems require balance.

In Italy, secrecy uses four national classification levels of increasing protection (Legge 124/2007): **RISERVATO (R)** - slight damage to the Republic's interests; **RISERVATISSIMO (RR)** - damage; **SEGRETO (S)** - serious damage; **SEGRETISSIMO (SS)** - exceptionally serious damage. These map internationally (e.g. SEGRETISSIMO ~ TOP SECRET; NATO uses COSMIC TOP SECRET / NATO SECRET / NATO CONFIDENTIAL / NATO RESTRICTED; 'NATO Unclassified' is not a classification but still entails minimum non-disclosure protection). Even with clearance, the **need-to-know** principle limits access to what is strictly necessary for a role or mission - *clearance is not unlimited access*; authorization and need-to-know work together to control dissemination.

blocks.title: Secrecy vs Confidentiality
blocks.text: Do not confuse them. **Confidentiality** is a **security property** (part of CIA) that protects data from unauthorized access, is implemented via technical mechanisms (encryption, access control) and applies to many systems. **Secrecy** is an **institutional/legal regime** that protects sensitive state interests, is implemented via **classification and handling rules**, and applies to specific categories of information.
5. Access control: AuthN vs AuthZ and the four models

Security properties must be *enforced* by technical and organizational mechanisms. **Access control** is the primary one: the process of **granting or denying specific requests** to obtain/use information and processing services, or to enter physical facilities (CNSSI 4009). It applies to both **logical and physical** domains and decides **who can access which resources, under which conditions, for which operations** - operationalizing confidentiality, integrity and accountability. Logically it is a **Subject -> Policy (PDP -> PEP) -> Object** pipeline: the **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** evaluates the applicable policy for that subject/object/operation and decides; the **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** enforces that decision.

- **Authentication (AuthN)** - *Who are you?* Verified with factors: something you **know** (password), **have** (token), **are** (biometrics). Combining them = **MFA**.
- **Authorization (AuthZ)** - *What are you allowed to do?*
- **Accountability** - *What did you do?* (logging).

blocks.title: Exam trap: authentication != authorization
blocks.text: Logging in proves **who you are** (AuthN). It does **not** decide **what you may do** (AuthZ). A correctly authenticated user can still be denied an action by the authorization policy. Mixing these up is one of the most common exam errors.

Each model differs in its **decision basis**:
- **DAC** (Discretionary Access Control) - identity of the subject and **ownership** of the object; owners grant access at their discretion.
- **MAC** (Mandatory Access Control) - **security labels and classification levels** (e.g. SECRET); set by central authority, not the owner.
- **RBAC** (Role-Based Access Control) - assigned **organizational roles** (e.g. 'nurse', 'professor').
- **ABAC** (Attribute-Based Access Control) - **attributes** of subject, object, action and **environment** (e.g. time, location).

question.prompt: A hospital system grants access based on rules like: 'a doctor may read a record IF the patient is assigned to them AND it is during a shift AND the request comes from inside the hospital network.' Which access control model is this?

options.content: DAC, because the doctor is identified
ABAC, because the decision combines attributes of subject, object, action and environment

question.explanation: The rule combines subject attributes (role=doctor, assignment), object attributes (the patient's record), action and **environmental** attributes (shift time, network location). Combining attributes including the environment is the signature of **ABAC**. Plain RBAC would decide on the role alone; DAC decides on identity/ownership; MAC decides on classification labels.
question.theory: ABAC decides on combined subject/object/action/environment attributes.

blocks.text: 6. How attacks work: threat chain, kill chain, CWE vs CVE

Attacks aim to **steal** (data, money, intellectual property, identity, credentials, privileged roles), **damage/disrupt** (services, critical infrastructure, logistics, healthcare, public administration, defense) or **manipulate/control** maliciously (alter decisions, falsify data, change system behavior, control physical processes). The course explains why attacks are possible with two drivers: **system complexity** (the threat chain) and the **nature of cyberspace** (asymmetry, jurisdiction, scale, anonymity, tool reuse).

Complexity manifests in three domains: **technological** (heterogeneous software stacks, third-party code, IT/OT interconnections), **organizational** (unclear responsibilities, supply-chain dependencies, inconsistent procedures) and **human** (errors, workarounds, social-engineering susceptibility). Complexity increases attack surface and misconfiguration probability - *a system can be secure in theory and insecure in practice because its real operational environment is too complex to control perfectly.*

The **security community** describes how attack opportunities arise with the **threat chain**. (Compare: the safety community uses Fault -> Error -> Failure for *accidental* causes; security uses an *adversarial* chain.)

**Weakness -> Vulnerability -> Exploit -> Attack**
- **Weakness** - a general, **classifiable** flaw in design, implementation, configuration or organizational practice (e.g. improper input validation, hard-coded password). It is the *class* of bug; it determines a system's **exposure** (the possibility of losing C, I or A).
- **Vulnerability** - that weakness made concrete in a specific component/version, that an attacker can exploit. A vulnerability *is one only if it is exploitable*: exploitability depends on **reachability** (Internet-facing vs internal vs isolated), **privileges/preconditions** (authentication, local access, existing foothold), **user interaction** (must the victim click/open/enable?) and **environmental mitigations** (MFA, segmentation, sandboxing, EDR, memory protections).
- **Exploit** - the means/method of taking advantage of a vulnerability: a **technical artifact** (code, payload, crafted input - e.g. a malicious document or crafted network packet) or an **operational technique**.
- **Attack** - a concrete set of **unauthorized actions** against an asset's C, I or A in order to take advantage of it (RFC 2828: malicious activity that attempts to harvest, disrupt, deny, degrade or destroy information-system resources).

Each link has a **defender response**: weakness -> secure design, reviews, architecture; vulnerability -> scanning, asset inventory, patching; exploitability -> hardening, segmentation, least privilege; attack -> detection, containment, recovery. Note the asymmetry of timing captured by the **window of exposure** (the period between a vulnerability existing and it being mitigated).

blocks.title: Real vulnerabilities (weakness becomes a concrete CVE)
blocks.text:
- **Heartbleed (OpenSSL, disclosed Apr 7 2014).** The TLS 'Heartbeat' keep-alive let a client send data plus a *declared* length; the server did not verify the actual message size, so it echoed back **extra memory contents** - leaking passwords, session cookies, private keys and user data from memory.
- **Log4Shell (Apache Log4j, disclosed Dec 9 2021).** Log4j's *lookup substitution* let crafted strings in a logged message trigger the library to contact an attacker-controlled server and **download and execute malicious code**.
- **EternalBlue (Microsoft Windows SMB, disclosed Apr 14 2017).** Improper validation of network-packet fields allowed a **memory-corruption** bug; crafted packets let an attacker inject and execute code **with no account or password**. It was used by the **WannaCry** ransomware to infect ~200,000-300,000 computers across more than 150 countries.

- **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration)** - a community-developed catalog of recurring software/hardware weakness **classes** (e.g. CWE-122 Heap-based Buffer Overflow). A common language and yardstick used mainly by **engineers to build** secure systems.
- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** - a list of specific, publicly disclosed **instances**, each with an ID, description and reference (e.g. CVE-2019-6778: a heap-based buffer overflow in QEMU 3.0.0). Used mainly by **defenders to defend** systems (identify whether they are exposed).

CWE = the class of bug (build secure systems); CVE = the instance of that bug in a real product/version (defend when vulnerabilities appear).

blocks.title: Exam trap: a CVE present does not mean exploitable in your environment
blocks.text: A CVE describes a vulnerability that exists *somewhere*. Whether it is exploitable for **you** depends on context: is the component Internet-facing, does it need authentication or user interaction, are there mitigations like MFA/EDR/segmentation? 'CVE present' does **not** automatically mean 'exploitable here'.

While the threat chain explains **why** systems are attackable, the **Cyber Kill Chain** (borrowed from military doctrine) explains **how** an attack is executed, as a sequence of stages. A kill chain can take from **minutes to years** to complete depending on the target's defenses, the target's preparedness, the resources of the attacker and the value of the target.
The seven phases of the Cyber Kill Chain (with where defenders can break it), illustrated by a campaign stealing IoT-company product designs:

steps.label: 1 Reconnaissance
steps.text: Gather technical info (exposed services, software versions, network ranges) and human/organizational info (employees, email formats, suppliers). **Passive** (websites, LinkedIn, GitHub, job postings, DNS, leaked credentials) vs **active** (scanning, host discovery, banner grabbing). Output: candidate entry points -> e.g. a spear-phishing target. Defend: exposure management, deception, monitoring.

steps.label: 2 Weaponization
steps.text: Prepare the malicious payload tailored to the target (malware, phishing pages, malicious documents, reused exploit kits). Output: a weaponized artifact - e.g. a phishing email with a malicious attachment. Defend: threat intelligence, attack-surface reduction, sandboxing/detonation.

steps.label: 3 Delivery
steps.text: Transmit the payload: phishing emails, malicious/drive-by websites, direct exploitation of exposed services, removable media, compromised supply chains, messaging, malvertising. Output: first contact - e.g. 'Updated_Design_Specifications.docx' reaches the victim's inbox via sender spoofing. Defend: email/web filtering, user training.

steps.label: 4 Exploitation
steps.text: The payload triggers a vulnerability or a user action so code executes - exploit software flaws, abuse misconfigurations, or use social engineering. Example: the engineer opens the document, **enables macros**, and a hidden macro downloads malware. Defend: patching, hardening, monitoring.

steps.label: 5 Installation
steps.text: Establish **persistence** (install a backdoor/RAT, create a startup task/service, hide via obfuscation). Output: persistent access and a platform for surveillance, lateral movement or data theft. Defend: application control, least privilege, monitoring.

steps.label: 6 Command & Control (C2)
steps.text: Malware connects to an attacker-controlled server (often over encrypted HTTPS), beacons periodically and can be integrated into botnets, giving the attacker interactive remote control. Defend: network detection, **egress control** (monitor/restrict outbound traffic).

steps.label: 7 Actions on Objectives
steps.text: Achieve the goal: data **exfiltration**, system manipulation/sabotage, fraud/extortion (ransomware), infrastructure abuse. Using the victim's legitimate access can make activity look normal at first. Result is a C breach, I compromise or A disruption. Defend: incident response, containment, eradication, recovery.

blocks.title: The kill chain is useful but not universal
blocks.text: It fits many intrusion-oriented campaigns but is less suitable for all attack types and adversary behaviours. Other models answer different questions: **attack trees** (how can a goal be achieved? - decompose an objective into alternative ways), **attack graphs** (what paths can an attacker follow through a system/network?), and **attack nets** (how does an attack progress through different states/conditions?). Good analysts use a model as a tool, not a rigid template.

- **State-sponsored (strategic).** Carried out by **APTs (Advanced Persistent Threats)** - adversaries with significant capability, resources and operational discipline, who operate over long periods, pursue strategic objectives, use multiple vectors (cyber + physical + deception) and adapt to defenses. Goals: **espionage** (intelligence collection), **disruption/sabotage** of critical services, **pre-positioning** (covert access maintained for future leverage), and **IP theft** to accelerate national industries. APT names are *labels for observed activity clusters*, not ground-truth identities (multiple naming complicates attribution).
- **Cybercriminal (profit).** Steal assets/credentials, extort (ransomware), sell stolen data/access, offer criminal services. It is an **ecosystem** with specialized roles (malware developers, phishers, **access brokers**, money mules) trading in underground markets - so operations **scale** because no single actor needs every skill.
- **Hacktivist (ideology).** Individuals or loosely coordinated collectives prioritizing **visibility over persistence**: website defacement, DDoS, data leaks, account hijacking, **doxing**. Impact is usually reputational/political rather than financial or strategic.

Cybercrime is criminal offences committed **using** computer systems/networks/data or that **target** them. Two broad categories: **cyber-enabled** crimes (traditional crime amplified by digital systems - online scams, fraud, identity theft) and **cyber-dependent** crimes (exist only because digital systems exist - malware, ransomware, botnets, DDoS-for-hire). It is formalized internationally by the Council of Europe **Budapest Convention (2001)**, which gives a shared legal baseline: common definitions of offences, international cooperation, and procedural measures (evidence preservation, lawful access). Its offence categories include: offences against the **CIA of systems/data** (unauthorized access/interception, data interference, system interference), **computer-related** offences (fraud, forgery) and **content/IP** offences. In Italy, frode informatica is covered by **art. 640-ter c.p.** Ethics/legality: *attacking a vulnerable system is a crime - a vulnerability does not create a right to exploit it; unauthorized access is illegal even with no damage* (you don't enter a house just because the door is open). Security testing requires explicit authorization (scope + rules of engagement).

blocks.title: High-impact ransomware incidents
blocks.text:
- **Colonial Pipeline (2021)** - the **DarkSide** group disrupted fuel distribution across the US East Coast and extorted a **\$4.4 million** ransom.
- **JBS (2021)** - the **REvil** group forced one of the world's largest meat processors to suspend operations at **13 US plants**; JBS paid an **\$11 million** Bitcoin ransom.
- **Costa Rica (2022)** - the **Conti** group entered via the Ministry of Finance and spread to many agencies, disrupting tax collection, payroll, customs and foreign trade; recovery took months.

Cybercrime's global cost (CSIS/CSIS estimates) has grown from about **\$445 billion (2014)** to about **\$600 billion (2018)** to about **\$1 trillion (2020)**. For critical services, **indirect losses (downtime + recovery) often dominate**. In Italy, official/national incident data come from **ACN**, **CSIRT Italia** and the annual **CLUSIT Report**.

**Cyberspace is a 'no man's land'** that favors attackers: there are no clear boundaries (an operation routes through devices, clouds, registrars and channels across many countries, so the 'location' of an attack is ambiguous), no clear **transnational jurisdiction** (victim, attacker, infrastructure and money flows can each be in different countries, complicating attribution and legal cooperation), and radically different social paradigms (no physical proximity needed, low cost to reach victims, large-scale targeting feasible). It is an **opportunity structure**: global reach at near-zero marginal cost, scalability via automation/botnets/tool reuse, low barriers to entry (commodity malware, exploit-kit markets, Crime-as-a-Service), operational anonymity, and rapid adaptation.

Cyberspace creates a structural asymmetry **in favor of attackers**:
- The **attacker needs only one viable path** and chooses the **time and place**; the **defender must cover every** asset, interface, user and configuration, **always-on**, and cannot pick convenient days.
- The attacker is **opportunistic, adaptive and unaccountable**; the defender is constrained by rules, accountability, internal escalation and coordination - which slows reaction.
- **Economic asymmetry:** attack infrastructure has mostly **fixed costs** and each extra victim adds **low marginal cost** (automation + reuse), so attacks scale cheaply; defenders face the opposite logic (each asset/control/monitoring requirement adds cost). This drives **Crime-as-a-Service (CaaS)**, where access, infrastructure, malware and distribution are rented - enabling attacks with less effort, lower expertise and relatively lower risk.
- **Information-sharing asymmetry:** attackers benefit from sharing tools/access (reuse lowers cost), while defenders face disincentives (reputational damage, liability, exposing weaknesses).

Because defenders face disincentives to share, **structured sharing mechanisms** are needed. **Shareable objects:** **IOCs (Indicators of Compromise)** - malicious IPs, domains, file hashes (artifacts of an intrusion); **TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures)** - attacker behaviours/methods; **YARA rules** - pattern-matching rules to detect malware; and recurring incident patterns. **Trusted channels:** **CSIRTs** (Computer Security Incident Response Teams), **ISACs** (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers), and sectoral/national communities, supported by **coordinated vulnerability disclosure**. **Formats/platforms:** **STIX** (machine-readable threat-intelligence format), **IODEF** (incident-reporting format) and **MISP** (collaborative threat-intelligence platform).

Defenses are classified by **actor and purpose**: **cyber-investigation** (law enforcement - attribution, digital forensics, takedowns), **cyber-intelligence** (intelligence community - situational awareness of actors, capabilities, intentions) and **cyber-defence** (military/national defense - protect missions, ensure resilience and response, deterrence and active defense). In **Italy's National Cyber Architecture** these map to four interdependent pillars: **Cybersecurity & resilience - ACN** (national policy/coordination, prevention, strategy); **Law enforcement - Polizia Postale / CNAIPIC** (investigation, digital evidence); **National cyber defence - Ministry of Defence / COR** (military security, cyber-domain operations, Defence ICT); and **Cyber intelligence - DIS, AISE, AISI** (threat assessment). Major incidents cut across pillars, so coordination is essential, with political-strategic alignment via the **Comitato Interministeriale per la Cybersicurezza (CIC)** and a broader ecosystem of research, citizens, private actors and EU/NATO partners. ACN also hosts the National Cybersecurity Certification Authority (with the **CVCN** evaluation/certification centre), the **NIS** national competent authority and single point of contact, **CSIRT Italia**, and the national coordination centre under the EU Cybersecurity Competence Centre.

blocks.title: Threat actors and the attacker-defender asymmetry
blocks.text:
Attacks are classified by **perpetrator**: **state-sponsored** (strategic; carried out by **APTs** - Advanced Persistent Threats), **cybercriminal** (profit; ransomware, Crime-as-a-Service), and **hacktivist** (ideology; visibility over persistence - defacements, DDoS, doxing).

Cyberspace creates a structural **asymmetry favoring attackers**: an attacker needs only *one* viable path and chooses the time and place; the defender must cover *every* asset, interface and user, always-on, and is constrained by rules and coordination.
7. MITRE ATT&CK and defensive mapping

The kill chain assumes a fairly **linear** intrusion, but real adversaries pivot between hosts, repeat and retry failed actions, re-establish persistence after containment, and reuse the same technique in different contexts. A defender may observe credential theft *before* obvious malware installation, lateral movement *without* a clear exploit phase, or persistence re-established after containment. So to make defense **measurable** we must describe **observable behavior**, not just phases. The kill chain asks *'where are we in the sequence?'* (good for progression, interruption, campaign structure); behavioral frameworks ask *'what is the adversary doing?'* (good for detection engineering, telemetry design, capability assessment, threat-informed defense). That is what MITRE ATT&CK provides - a **behavioral ontology**.

A **curated, continuously updated knowledge base of real-world adversary behavior**, built from documented observations of intrusion campaigns. It does **not** primarily catalog malware families, vulnerabilities or specific tools - it catalogs **behavioral patterns**, at three levels:
- **Tactic** - the *strategic objective*, *why* an action is performed (the adversary's immediate objective, e.g. Initial Access, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Credential Access, Lateral Movement, Command and Control, Impact). These are the **columns** of the matrix.
- **Technique** - the *operational method*, *how* the objective is achieved (a reusable method, e.g. OS Credential Dumping). A good technique is **observable, reusable across campaigns, not tied to one malware family, and mappable to telemetry/controls**. These are the **cells** (with finer **sub-techniques**).
- **Procedure** - the *concrete implementation* in a specific campaign (e.g. dumping **LSASS** memory with a particular tool, or introducing an infected USB into an air-gapped engineering workstation).

blocks.title: ATT&CK is not another kill chain
blocks.text: The matrix is **not** read left-to-right as a mandatory sequence. It is a behavioral **space**: multiple techniques can serve the same tactic, the same technique can appear in very different campaigns, and attackers traverse it many ways. (Example: for **Credential Access** an adversary might use brute force, phishing, credential dumping, password-store access, or adversary-in-the-middle - same objective, different behaviors.) Read columns as goals and **select techniques from observed behavior**. Analyst workflow: *observe behavior -> map to technique -> infer objective.* Behavioral overlap between campaigns is informative but **not enough alone for attribution.**

A technique is **defensible only if it is observable**. The defensive chain is:

**Technique -> Artifact -> Telemetry -> Detection logic -> Response**

An **artifact** is a trace left by an action (process creation, registry modification, file change, network connection, authentication event, configuration change). **Telemetry** is the mechanism that captures it (OS logs, endpoint sensors, network monitoring, application logs, industrial monitoring). **Detection is not mere logging** - it requires a behavioral hypothesis, a baseline of normal activity, correlation across sources, false-positive reduction and prioritization. Example: *OS Credential Dumping* leaves the **artifact** of abnormal access to LSASS memory; **telemetry** (endpoint process monitoring) captures it; **detection logic** flags a suspicious non-system process reading LSASS; the **response** isolates the host and acquires forensic evidence. Without telemetry, artifacts exist but stay invisible.

blocks.title: Defensive mapping as an analytical workflow
blocks.text: ATT&CK becomes operational only when connected to telemetry and control decisions, via four steps:

steps.label: 1 Select techniques
steps.text: Which ATT&CK techniques matter for our threat model?

steps.label: 2 Assess visibility
steps.text: What telemetry do we collect that could reveal those techniques?

steps.label: 3 Assess detection
steps.text: Do we actually detect them reliably and with acceptable false positives?

steps.label: 4 Assess mitigation & residual risk
steps.text: If detection fails or arrives late, what controls still limit damage?

blocks.title: Coverage is layered, not binary - and residual risk remains
blocks.text: Coverage must be measured along three dimensions: **visibility** (do we collect relevant telemetry?), **detection** (do we identify the technique reliably?) and **mitigation** (if it succeeds, do controls reduce impact?). A technique may be *visible but not detected*, *detected but too late*, *detected but not mitigated*, or *mitigated but still partially exploitable*. The portion of adversary behavior that stays unobserved, undetected, insufficiently mitigated or still damaging is **residual risk**, arising from telemetry blind spots, weak analytical coverage, delayed response, incomplete mitigation and attacker adaptation. Each technique ultimately threatens one or more **security properties** (confidentiality - dumping/exfiltration; integrity - logic/data manipulation; availability - destruction/sabotage; authenticity - masquerading/deceptive reporting). In national defense, ATT&CK gives a **shared vocabulary**, standardized reporting, comparable defensive posture and threat-informed capability planning across agencies.

blocks.title: Case study: Stuxnet (IT + OT + physical)
blocks.text:
Discovered in **2010**, Stuxnet was the first widely recognized cyber operation to cause **physical sabotage** of industrial equipment through software - targeting **uranium-enrichment centrifuges at Natanz** by manipulating Siemens **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** logic. Natanz relied mostly on **physical isolation / air-gapping** rather than strong internal cyber defenses, so Stuxnet was *built for that constraint*: it entered via **human-carried infected removable media** (most plausibly a maintenance engineer's USB), used **multiple Windows zero-days** to propagate locally, checked for specific **Siemens Step7/PLC** conditions, and activated its payload **only when the target industrial configuration matched**. It then altered centrifuge rotation speeds in ways designed to **stress the equipment** gradually while **replaying fake normal readings** to operators to mask the abnormality.

Kill Chain view (structural): Recon (gather intel on PLC families/centrifuge profiles) -> Weaponization (zero-days + custom PLC payload) -> Delivery (USB to an air-gapped workstation) -> Exploitation (code execution + propagation) -> Installation (Windows persistence + PLC logic injection) -> C2 (spread/update configuration) -> Actions on Objective (alter centrifuge speed, fake readings). ATT&CK view (behavioral): Initial Access = **Replication Through Removable Media**; Execution = **Exploitation for Client Execution**; Persistence = **Boot or Logon Autostart Execution**; Privilege Escalation = **Exploitation for Privilege Escalation** (SYSTEM); Lateral Movement = **Exploitation of Remote Services** (e.g. Print Spooler); Defense Evasion = **Rootkit**; C2 = **Application Layer Protocol** (HTTP when connectivity existed); Impact = **Modify Controller Logic**.

Five enduring lessons: (1) air-gapping **reduces exposure but does not remove all attack paths**; (2) **IT and OT security cannot be separated** in industrial defense; (3) incomplete telemetry means structurally weak visibility; (4) **behavioral monitoring** is essential in industrial environments; (5) cyber operations can produce **strategic physical effects** supporting national objectives.

question.prompt: In MITRE ATT&CK, 'Credential Access' is achieved in one campaign by brute force and in another by credential dumping. What does this illustrate, and how does ATT&CK differ from the Cyber Kill Chain here?

options.content: It shows a fixed sequence; ATT&CK and the kill chain are the same thing
'Credential Access' is a tactic (an objective) that multiple techniques can satisfy; ATT&CK is a behavioral space, not a mandatory sequence
Brute force and credential dumping are tactics; the kill chain lists them as phases
It shows that ATT&CK catalogs malware families rather than behaviors

question.explanation: 'Credential Access' is a **tactic** (the *why*), and brute force and credential dumping are two **techniques** (the *how*) that both achieve it - same objective, different behaviors. Unlike the kill chain's linear phases, ATT&CK is a behavioral **space** traversed many ways. Brute force/dumping are techniques, not tactics (rules out C); ATT&CK deliberately catalogs **behaviors**, not malware families (rules out D); and it is explicitly **not** a fixed sequence (rules out A).
question.theory: A tactic is the objective; many techniques satisfy it - ATT&CK is a behavioral space, not a sequence.

blocks.title: Module A in one breath
blocks.text: Cyberspace is a systemic socio-technical system, so security must be reasoned about with a shared vocabulary: protect **assets** from **threats** that exploit **vulnerabilities**, quantified as **risk = severity x probability**. Separate **safety / security / dependability** and prefer **(safety & security)-by-design** with fault-tolerant and vulnerability-tolerant architectures, aiming at **resilience**. Enforce the **CIA** triad (plus authenticity, non-repudiation, accountability) using crypto (encryption, MACs, signatures; Kerckhoffs) and **access control** (AuthN vs AuthZ; DAC/MAC/RBAC/ABAC). Respect **privacy / data-protection (GDPR) / secrecy** distinctions, with anonymization vs pseudonymization. Describe attacks with the **threat chain** (weakness->vulnerability->exploit->attack, CWE vs CVE), the **kill chain** (how), threat-actor classes and real incidents, and **MITRE ATT&CK** (observable behavior: tactics/techniques/procedures) to build measurable, threat-informed defense - all coordinated through a national cyber architecture and structured intelligence sharing.
